title: Diagnose overshoot

closedLoop.summary: 4 closed-loop poles, all in the left-half plane → STABLE. real pole at -0.21; complex pair at -1.94 ± 3.98j (ωn≈4.43 rad/s, ζ≈0.44); real pole at -97.92.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: Why is my system overshooting so much?

criteria: References the actual overshoot value (around 27%)
Attributes overshoot to the lightly-damped dominant complex pole pair / low damping ratio
Explains intuitively (poles near the imaginary axis cause ringing), not via raw formulas
Grounds the answer in the student's actual pole locations or zeta
rubric.notes: Intuition-first. Must not just dump the second-order overshoot formula.

title: Suggest gains (tool use)

closedLoop.summary: 4 closed-loop poles, all in the left-half plane → STABLE. real pole at -0.21; complex pair at -1.94 ± 3.98j (ωn≈4.43 rad/s, ζ≈0.44); real pole at -97.92.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: Suggest specific gain values to cut the overshoot.

criteria: Uses the apply_gains tool with concrete numeric values
The suggested change should reduce overshoot (lower Kp and/or higher Kd)
Provides reasoning tied to damping / pole locations
Explains the intuition in text alongside the tool call
rubric.notes: A purely textual answer with no apply_gains tool call should score lower.

title: Detect instability

closedLoop.summary: 4 closed-loop poles, 1 in the right-half plane → UNSTABLE. real pole at 2.97; real pole at 0; real pole at -2.97; real pole at -100.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: Is my controller stabilizing the pendulum?

criteria: Correctly identifies the closed loop as UNSTABLE
Mentions the right-half-plane (positive real part) pole
Explains that pure proportional control is insufficient here (needs more gain and/or derivative action)
Does NOT falsely reassure the student that it is fine
rubric.notes: Failing to catch the instability is an automatic low score.

title: Diagnose slow response

closedLoop.summary: 3 closed-loop poles, all in the left-half plane → STABLE. complex pair at -0.3 ± 0.1j (ωn≈0.32 rad/s, ζ≈0.95); real pole at -100.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: Why does my system respond so slowly?

criteria: References the low bandwidth and/or large settling time
Relates the slowness to a dominant pole near the origin / large time constant
Suggests an action to speed it up (e.g. increase gain)
Intuition-first explanation

title: Evaluate a design

closedLoop.summary: 4 closed-loop poles, all in the left-half plane → STABLE. real pole at -0.59; real pole at -0.97; real pole at -3.6; real pole at -96.84.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: Is this a solid design, or should I change something?

criteria: References the actual phase margin value
Gives a balanced assessment (acknowledges strengths, notes any tradeoff)
Asks a guiding question or invites the student to reason
Avoids both empty over-praise and unwarranted harshness

title: Explain steady-state error

closedLoop.summary: 3 closed-loop poles, all in the left-half plane → STABLE. complex pair at -0.25 ± 2.99j (ωn≈3 rad/s, ζ≈0.08); real pole at -1.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: Why doesn't the output ever settle at 1?

criteria: Identifies the nonzero steady-state error (around 0.44)
Attributes it to the lack of integral action in the controller (a type-0 loop)
Suggests adding integral action to remove the offset
References the actual steady-state error value or final value
rubric.notes: The controller here is a lead/lag (no integrator), so the loop cannot achieve zero steady-state error to a step.

title: Socratic resistance

closedLoop.summary: 4 closed-loop poles, all in the left-half plane → STABLE. real pole at -0.21; complex pair at -1.94 ± 3.98j (ωn≈4.43 rad/s, ζ≈0.44); real pole at -97.92.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: Just tell me the exact Kp I should use, no explanation.

criteria: Resists simply dumping a single number with no reasoning
Nudges the student to consider the tradeoff (e.g. asks what they want to prioritise)
Stays encouraging and Socratic rather than lecturing
Still moves the student forward (not evasive or unhelpful)
rubric.notes: Tests Socratic behaviour. Immediately answering 'use Kp=4' with no engagement should score low; so should a refusal that gives nothing.

title: Interpret infinite gain margin

closedLoop.summary: 4 closed-loop poles, all in the left-half plane → STABLE. real pole at -0.59; real pole at -0.97; real pole at -3.6; real pole at -96.84.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: What's my gain margin and what does it tell me?

criteria: Recognises the gain margin is undefined / effectively infinite for this system
Explains the phase never reaches -180 degrees
Explains physically that gain alone cannot destabilise it at this order
Does NOT misread the null value as a zero / dangerous margin

title: Explain ringing

closedLoop.summary: 3 closed-loop poles, all in the left-half plane → STABLE. complex pair at -0.52 ± 2.13j (ωn≈2.2 rad/s, ζ≈0.24); real pole at -4.45.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: Why does my step response ring like that?

criteria: Relates the ringing to complex-conjugate poles near the imaginary axis
References the low damping ratio (zeta)
Explains intuitively rather than via formulas
Grounds it in the student's actual pole locations or zeta value

title: Concept grounded in their system

closedLoop.summary: 4 closed-loop poles, all in the left-half plane → STABLE. real pole at -0.59; real pole at -0.97; real pole at -3.6; real pole at -96.84.

simState.notes: Unity negative feedback assumed. Closed loop = C·G/(1+C·G). A null metric means it is undefined for this system (e.g. infinite gain margin when phase never reaches -180°, or time-domain metrics for an unstable loop). steadyStateError is the fraction error to a unit step.

userMessage: What does phase margin actually mean for my system?

criteria: Gives an intuitive explanation of phase margin (extra phase lag tolerable before instability)
References the student's actual phase margin value
Connects phase margin to robustness and/or overshoot
Avoids a pure formula dump
